codeSnapshot.label: datetime_features.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'], utc=True)

# ── 1. Calendar decomposition ──
df['year']        = df['timestamp'].dt.year
df['month']       = df['timestamp'].dt.month        # 1–12
df['day_of_week'] = df['timestamp'].dt.dayofweek    # 0=Monday, 6=Sunday
df['day_of_month']= df['timestamp'].dt.day          # 1–31
df['hour']        = df['timestamp'].dt.hour         # 0–23
df['quarter']     = df['timestamp'].dt.quarter      # 1–4
df['is_weekend']  = (df['day_of_week'] >= 5).astype(int)

# ── 2. Cyclical encoding (prevent edge discontinuity) ──
# Month 12 and month 1 are adjacent — treating month as integer implies
# |12 - 1| = 11 (far apart), but they are only 1 month apart cyclically
df['month_sin'] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df['month'] / 12)
df['month_cos'] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df['month'] / 12)
df['dow_sin']   = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df['day_of_week'] / 7)
df['dow_cos']   = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df['day_of_week'] / 7)
df['hour_sin']  = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df['hour'] / 24)
df['hour_cos']  = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df['hour'] / 24)

# ── 3. Lag features (for time series / sequential data) ──
df = df.sort_values(['user_id', 'timestamp'])
df['spend_lag_1d']  = df.groupby('user_id')['spend'].shift(1)   # previous day
df['spend_lag_7d']  = df.groupby('user_id')['spend'].shift(7)   # 1 week ago
df['days_since_last_purchase'] = (
    df.groupby('user_id')['timestamp']
    .diff()  # timedelta
    .dt.days
)

# ── 4. Rolling window aggregates ──
df = df.sort_values(['user_id', 'timestamp'])
df['spend_7d_sum']  = df.groupby('user_id')['spend'].transform(
    lambda x: x.shift(1).rolling(7).sum()  # shift(1): exclude current row
)
df['spend_30d_mean'] = df.groupby('user_id')['spend'].transform(
    lambda x: x.shift(1).rolling(30).mean()
)

# ── 5. Elapsed time since reference event ──
df['account_age_days'] = (df['timestamp'] - df['signup_date']).dt.days
df['days_to_deadline'] = (df['deadline'] - df['timestamp']).dt.days

eli3: Dates and timestamps contain rich information, but a raw date like '2024-06-15' is just a string — a model can't automatically know it's a Saturday in June. Date/time feature engineering extracts useful signals: which day of the week, which hour, is it a weekend, how many days since the last purchase?

A tricky problem: cyclical features. Month 12 (December) and month 1 (January) are one month apart, but as integers they're 11 apart. Using month as a raw number tells the model they're far apart, which is wrong. The solution is to encode cyclical features as sine and cosine pairs, which wraps the scale so that December and January are correctly represented as adjacent.

For sequential data (user transactions over time), lag features and rolling window aggregates are the most powerful date-based features: how much did this user spend in the last 7 days? How many days since their last purchase? These features capture recency and frequency signals — key in churn prediction, fraud detection, and recommendation.

whenWhy.title: Time series forecasting
whenWhy.body: For forecasting sales, energy demand, or traffic, temporal features encode seasonality: month captures annual seasonality, day-of-week captures weekly patterns, hour captures intraday patterns. Together with lag features (value yesterday) and rolling averages (value averaged over last week), they provide the temporal context the model needs. Libraries like Prophet and statsmodels encode seasonality implicitly; for custom models (LightGBM on tabular format), you build these features manually.

whenWhy.title: User behaviour modelling
whenWhy.body: Churn prediction, credit scoring, and fraud detection use recency-frequency-monetary (RFM) features — all derived from timestamps: days since last purchase (recency), number of purchases in last 90 days (frequency), total spend in last 90 days (monetary). These are among the most predictive features in most B2C ML applications. Rolling window aggregates (shift(1).rolling(k)) compute these correctly without looking into the future.

whenWhy.title: Event timing features
whenWhy.body: Elapsed time since a reference event is often highly predictive: account age (days since signup → new vs established user), days since last login (engagement signal), days remaining until contract renewal (upsell window). These are computed as timestamp differences using pd.Timedelta and .dt.days. Business events (product launches, marketing campaigns, holidays) can be encoded as binary 'in-campaign' flags.

deepDiveIntro: Cyclical encoding represents periodic variables (hour of day, day of week, month) as a pair of sine and cosine values. This places each time point on a circle, so that the end of a cycle is correctly recognized as adjacent to the beginning — December sits next to January, and 11pm sits next to midnight. Raw integer encoding places them far apart, misleading linear and distance-based models. [1]

Lag features capture the value from k steps in the past; rolling window features smooth over the last k values. The most critical rule: always shift by 1 before rolling so that the feature at time t only uses data from before t. Forgetting the shift leaks the current value into its own predictor, a form of target leakage that inflates validation scores. [2]

deepDive.heading: Cyclical Encoding: Why and How
deepDive.body: If you encode hour as an integer from 0 to 23, a model treats hour 23 and hour 0 as 23 units apart — but they're only one hour apart on the clock. Sine-cosine encoding maps each hour to a point on the unit circle, so that adjacent hours are close in Euclidean distance regardless of the wrap-around. Tree models can work around this by making two separate splits, so cyclical encoding matters most for linear models, neural networks, and distance-based methods. [1]

deepDive.heading: Lag and Rolling Window Features
deepDive.body: Lag features capture the persistence of a signal: the most recent past value is usually the best predictor of the next one. Using multiple lag windows (1, 7, 14, 28 days) captures both short-term and seasonal patterns. Rolling averages smooth out noise over a window of past values. Exponential moving averages (EMA) weight recent values more heavily than older ones. Always sort by entity and timestamp before computing lags within groups, and use .transform() to correctly align the resulting column back to the original index. [2]

deepDive.heading: Avoiding Leakage in Temporal Features
deepDive.body: Rolling aggregates without a prior shift include the current observation, so the feature at time t uses information from t itself — a leak. Always apply shift(1) before rolling. Incorrect sort order can also cause future values to appear as lags of past ones. Global statistics computed on the full dataset — even seemingly innocent ones like rolling means — include future observations if not handled carefully. For a true cumulative historical average, use expanding().mean() rather than a fixed rolling window. [2]

youtubeLabel: Time Series Feature Engineering — Rob Mulla
takeaways: Cyclical encoding: sin(2πt/T) and cos(2πt/T) pair. Preserves wrap-around topology. Essential for linear/distance models; less critical for trees. [1]
Lag features: x_{t−k} captures persistence. Rolling aggregates: shift(1).rolling(k).mean() captures local trends. shift(1) prevents leakage. [2]
RFM features (recency, frequency, monetary) from timestamps are among the strongest predictors in user behaviour modelling. [2]
Elapsed time: (timestamp − reference).dt.days gives account age, time since last event, time to deadline. [1]
Critical: sort by (entity_id, timestamp) before lag/rolling. Use time-based train/test splits to prevent future data from influencing training. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle daylight saving time (DST) and time zone conversion in datetime features?
edgeCases.a: Store all timestamps in UTC — DST transitions (when clocks spring forward or fall back) create ambiguous local times. If your data source provides local times, convert to UTC at ingestion time: pd.to_datetime(col, utc=True) or .dt.tz_localize('America/New_York').dt.tz_convert('UTC'). For feature extraction: compute hour/day-of-week from local time in the user's time zone (users behave according to local time, not UTC). Use .dt.tz_convert('America/New_York').dt.hour to get local hour. Never compute time differences or rolling windows on tz-naive timestamps — subtract first, then extract .dt.days or .dt.total_seconds().

edgeCases.q: How do you create temporal features for non-uniform time series (events don't occur at regular intervals)?
edgeCases.a: For irregular event streams (transactions, clicks, support tickets), timestamps are not on a regular grid. Two strategies: (1) Reindex to a regular grid — resample to daily/hourly with .resample().agg() — filling gaps with 0 or NaN. This converts to regular time series and enables standard lag/rolling features. (2) Event-based features — for each event, compute: days since last event of same type, count of events in last [7, 30, 90] days, rate of events per day over last period. These don't require resampling and are robust to irregular spacing. The correct approach depends on whether you need time-series models (ARIMA, Transformer-TS — needs regular grid) or tabular ML (LightGBM, XGBoost — event-based features work directly).

sources.label: Hyndman, R.J. & Athanasopoulos, G. — Forecasting: Principles and Practice, 3rd Ed. (OTexts, 2021; free online)
McKinney, W. — Python for Data Analysis, 3rd Ed., Ch. 11 (O'Reilly, 2022)
